{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics system with an established invariant that every Shipment has exactly one assigned Carrier, with Supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests automatic splitting of heavy shipments (>500kg) into sub-shipments assigned to different carriers without Supervisor involvement.",

    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "A unit of goods to be transported, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier." },
      { "term": "Carrier", "definition": "The party responsible for physically transporting a Shipment." },
      { "term": "Carrier Assignment", "definition": "The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Currently locked once set; only a Supervisor can reassign." },
      { "term": "Supervisor", "definition": "A privileged role authorized to override Carrier assignments." },
      { "term": "Sub-Shipment", "definition": "PROPOSED — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment, each retaining the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant." },
      { "term": "Auto-Split", "definition": "PROPOSED — automatic decomposition of a Shipment exceeding a weight threshold into multiple Sub-Shipments, each independently assigned to a Carrier." },

    ]

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times after assignment.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a state integrity constraint. If a Shipment has zero or multiple Carriers, accountability is undefined — you cannot determine who is responsible for loss, damage, or delay. The system's correctness depends on this 1:1 binding.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any Shipment in state 'assigned' or later, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Carrier reassignment on a Shipment requires Supervisor authorization.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an access control policy. A VP could decide to relax this to dispatcher-level authorization. The system would not be corrupt — just governed differently.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an associated Supervisor authorization record."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Physical constraint — a parcel on a truck must be the responsibility of exactly one carrier. Without this, chain-of-custody breaks down and liability is unassignable.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Shipment can transition to 'dispatched' state unless it has exactly one assigned Carrier."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

    { "scenario": "No available carriers for one of the sub-shipments", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — does the entire split fail atomically, or can partial splits exist?" },
    { "scenario": "Parent shipment has already been assigned a carrier before split triggers", "expected_behavior": "CONTRADICTION — splitting reassigns/removes the original carrier without Supervisor involvement, violating BR-2." },
    { "scenario": "Customer tracks shipment and sees it fragment into multiple sub-shipments", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — what is the customer-facing experience? One tracking number or many? Who is accountable to the customer?" },
    { "scenario": "Sub-shipment is damaged in transit", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — liability and claims process: does the customer file against the parent shipment or the sub-shipment's carrier?" },
    { "scenario": "Billing for a split shipment", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — is the customer charged once for the original shipment or per sub-shipment? Who absorbs the cost of using multiple carriers?" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Splitting shipments across multiple carriers likely increases total shipping cost (loss of volume discounts, multiple pickup/delivery fees). Without clear billing rules, revenue leakage or customer disputes are likely.",
    "operational": "Introduces a parent-child shipment relationship that does not exist in the current model. Every downstream system (tracking, billing, claims, reporting, SLA monitoring) must understand this new relationship or produce incorrect results.",
    "user": "Customers expecting a single delivery may receive multiple partial deliveries at different times. This changes the delivery experience fundamentally and could reduce satisfaction if not communicated clearly.",

      { "group": "Supervisors", "impact": "Bypassed by auto-split for carrier assignment — their control authority is undermined for a class of shipments." },

    ]
  },

  "confidence": {

Cannot define acceptance criteria until the contradiction is resolved and missing context is provided.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant is real and must be preserved or explicitly renegotiated",

      "The 500kg threshold is a business policy, not a physical constraint",
      "Product team may not have been aware of the carrier assignment invariant when writing this request"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The auto-split feature directly contradicts BR-1 and BR-2. A single Shipment cannot have multiple Carriers — that is a production invariant.       "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Team", "Engineering", "Supervisors", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Introduce a 'Sub-Shipment' aggregate: the parent Shipment is split into N child Sub-Shipments, each preserving the one-Carrier invariant. The parent becomes a logical grouping, not a dispatchable entity. This preserves BR-1 but requires defining the parent-child lifecycle.",
        "B) Redefine the Shipment aggregate to allow multi-carrier assignment for split shipments, breaking BR-1. This is a foundational model change with system-wide impact.",
        "C) Require Supervisor approval for auto-split (system proposes the split, Supervisor confirms). This preserves BR-2 but contradicts the 'without supervisor involvement' requirement."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — introduce Sub-Shipment as a first-class domain concept. This resolves the contradiction without breaking the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant. Each Sub-Shipment is a real Shipment with its own Carrier. The parent Shipment becomes a 'Shipment Group' or 'Composite Shipment' that tracks the whole. Supervisor bypass for the *initial* auto-split could be modeled as a system-initiated action (distinct from human reassignment), preserving the intent of BR-2."

      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The feature request does not specify: (1) billing model for split shipments, (2) customer-facing tracking experience, (3) liability/claims handling across sub-shipments, (4) whether the split is atomic or can partially fail, (5) how the 500kg threshold interacts with item-level indivisibility (what if one item is 600kg?).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Team", "Finance", "Customer Support", "Legal"],
      "options": [
        "Product team provides a complete spec covering billing, tracking, liability, and atomicity before implementation begins",
        "Engineering implements the split mechanic only, deferring billing/tracking/liability to follow-up work"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Full spec required before implementation. Billing and liability are not deferrable — they determine the shape of the domain model. Building the split mechanic without knowing the billing model will likely require a rewrite."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
